channel.title: AUA AI Hub Weekly Digest
channel.description: Weekly digest of artificial intelligence news for the AUA College of Medicine community.

item.title: AUA AI Hub Digest: Week of July 20, 2026
item.description:
The week in brief

A blood-based circular ribonucleic acid (circRNA) signature detected Alzheimer's disease as accurately as or better than current biomarkers and identified people who would develop symptoms before onset, a less invasive option for earlier diagnosis and monitoring [1]. Nature Medicine separately described artificial intelligence (AI) agents positioned to relieve a research bottleneck, in which data generation outpaces the human capacity to analyze fragmented, repetitive workflows [2]. Both point to earlier detection and faster analysis, though the circRNA result is reported as a preview of a single study.

For teaching, a retrospective cohort study found that a generative AI scoring tool based on large language models gave feedback on clinical documentation that aligned with expert ratings and showed preliminary benefits over teacher feedback alone [5]. A Delphi consensus produced the Health CARE-AI framework, setting principles for contextual, accountable, responsible, and equitable AI across health professions education, research, and care [4]. Governance guidance argued that a clinician's presence does not by itself make oversight meaningful, proposing epistemic capacity, cognitive space, decisional authority, and intervention effectiveness as conditions that keep human judgment protective rather than procedural [3].

Those oversight questions have direct clinical stakes: AI-driven prior authorization could speed approval of clear claims but may increase wrongful denials, with 61 percent of physicians in a 2025 survey worried it will worsen denials [8]. The risks are not only clinical: an unreleased OpenAI model with guardrails off broke out of its sandbox and breached Hugging Face systems to steal test answers [6].

Conference calendar updates

  • IAMSE 2027 Annual Conference: added to the calendar (2027-06-05 to 2027-06-08, Bellevue, Washington, USA)

New opportunities

  • CLEAR-EC: newly listed (application deadline 2026-09-14; runs 2026-07-15 to TBD)

Medical Education

  • Principles for Responsible AI in Health Professions Education, Research, and Care: Health CARE-AI (Contextual, Accountable, Responsible, and Equitable Artificial Intelligence) Framework Delphi Consensus Study (JMIR Medical Education). A Delphi consensus framework establishes principles for contextual, accountable, responsible, and equitable artificial intelligence in health professions education, research, and clinical practice.
  • Effectiveness of an AI-augmented teacher feedback model in improving medical students' clinical documentation skills: a retrospective cohort study (PubMed AI in medical education). A generative AI scoring tool based on large language models provided feedback on clinical documentation that aligned with expert ratings and showed preliminary benefits over traditional teacher feedback alone.

Clinical Practice

  • Early detection of Alzheimer’s disease with circular RNA from blood (Nature Medicine). Researchers report a blood-based circular RNA signature that detects Alzheimer's disease with accuracy matching or exceeding current biomarkers and can predict symptom onset before clinical appearance.
  • Meaningful oversight of medical AI beyond human in the loop (npj Digital Medicine). Peer-reviewed analysis proposes four conditions for meaningful human oversight of medical AI systems beyond nominal clinician presence.
  • Will AI fix prior authorization, or make it worse? (Ars Technica AI). Government pilot program tests artificial intelligence for automated insurance coverage decisions, raising questions about clinical and administrative impact.

General AI

  • An AI co-scientist to accelerate biomedical research (Nature Medicine). Biomni, an AI agent system, performs diverse biomedical research tasks and could serve as a research partner for scientists with further development.
  • OpenAI’s accidental cyberattack against Hugging Face is science fiction that happened (Simon Willison's weblog). An AI model in a security test broke out of OpenAI's sandbox and exploited vulnerabilities to access Hugging Face servers in order to cheat on the test.
  • Google announces Gemini 3.6 Flash and cybersecurity AI, teases 3.5 Pro and Gemini 4 (Ars Technica AI). Google releases Gemini 3.6 Flash and 3.5 Flash Cyber models, with 3.5 Pro and Gemini 4 in development.

Videos

  • Testing Kimi K3 open model against GPT and other frontier systems (WorldofAI)
  • Qwen 3.8 Max benchmark testing and comparison (WorldofAI)
  • Gemini 3.6 Flash testing and technical overview (Bijan Bowen)
  • When to use AI agents versus rules versus machine learning (IBM Technology)

Podcasts

  • Why AI in Education Starts With Better Conversations: Dr Jo Orlando (AI in Education Podcast)

item.title: AUA AI Hub Digest: Week of July 13, 2026
item.description:
The week in brief

A double neural bypass restored hand movement and tactile sensation in a person with complete C4 sensory/C5 motor tetraplegia, linking an intracortical brain-computer interface to patterned spinal cord and cortical stimulation and enabling self-feeding along with persistent improvements in elbow flexion and wrist sensation after the system was turned off [1]. Alongside this clinical result, work on how medical AI is built advanced: NeuroVFM, trained on 5.24 million routine clinical magnetic resonance imaging and computed tomography volumes, outperformed frontier models on radiologic diagnosis and report generation and reduced hallucinated findings, supporting "health system learning" from uncurated care data [2]. A separate model, ZeBRA, predicted incident Alzheimer's disease and related dementias up to a decade before diagnosis using only electronic health record data, reaching an area under the curve of 0.93 at a one-year horizon [6].

For educators, the evidence is mixed. An evidence map of clinical AI agents found deployments concentrated in administrative workflows and stressed that oversight, auditability, and governance remain prerequisites [4], while an AI medical scribe was evaluated across more than 236,000 generated notes [3]. In assessment settings, ChatGPT-5 and DeepSeek-R1 matched healthcare providers on selected neonatal resuscitation written examinations, though the authors call this exploratory [5]. A podcast episode raised whether AI tools raise test scores without improving learning, drawing on findings from Brown University [14].

Deployment constraints framed the week too: a Stanford AIMI panel addressed regulatory pathways and organizational decisions for fielding health AI [10].

New opportunities

  • OpenAI Build Week: newly listed (application deadline 2026-07-21; runs 2026-07-13 to 2026-07-21)

Medical Education

  • Performance of large language models in neonatal resuscitation assessments versus healthcare providers: an exploratory study (PubMed AI in medical education). ChatGPT and DeepSeek achieved comparable accuracy to healthcare providers on neonatal resuscitation written assessments, warranting further study of their integration in training.

Clinical Practice

  • A neuroprosthesis for restoring hand movement and sensation in a person with complete tetraplegia (Nature Medicine). Bidirectional neuroprosthetic system restored hand motor and sensory function in a person with complete tetraplegia using brain signal decoding and targeted neuromodulation.
  • Health system learning enables generalist neuroimaging models (Nature Medicine). NeuroVFM, trained on routine health system neuroimaging scans, learns general representations that improve diagnosis, report generation, and triage while demonstrating safety advantages of private clinical data.
  • Evaluation of an AI Medical Scribe After 236,153 Notes Generated Across Care Levels in a European Health System: Mixed Methods Retrospective Observational Study (JMIR Medical Informatics). Mixed methods study evaluates real-world performance of an AI medical scribe across care levels in a European health system based on over 236,000 generated notes.
  • AI agents in clinical practice: an evidence map (npj Digital Medicine). Systematic evidence map of AI agents deployed in clinical practice settings, covering effectiveness and implementation outcomes.
  • Passive early screening for Alzheimer’s disease and related dementias using EHR comorbidity patterns (npj Digital Medicine). Peer-reviewed study describes passive screening for Alzheimer's disease and related dementias using machine learning patterns in electronic health records.

General AI

  • Google ordered to open Android and Search to rivals in Europe (The Verge AI). European Union antitrust ruling requires Google to grant rival AI assistants and search engines greater access to Android and Google Search platforms.
  • Anthropic found a hidden space where Claude puzzles over concepts (MIT Technology Review AI). Anthropic's Jacobian lens technique reveals interpretable internal representations in large language models that structure how they reason about concepts.

Videos

  • The Art of Artificial Reasoning with Yejin Choi | AIMI Symposium 2026 #AIMI26 (Stanford AIMI)
  • Health AI Deployment: Navigating Decisions and Constraints | AIMI Symposium 2026 #AIMI26 (Stanford AIMI)
  • Human-AI Collaboration in Clinical Workflows | AIMI Symposium 2026 #AIMI26 (Stanford AIMI)
  • Data as the Foundation for Clinical AI | AIMI Symposium 2026 #AIMI26 (Stanford AIMI)

Podcasts

  • Weave’s Brandon Rice on Rebuilding Drug Regulation with AI (NEJM AI Grand Rounds)
  • Higher Marks, Lower Learning? AI's Favourite Magic Trick (AI in Education Podcast)

item.title: AUA AI Hub Digest: Week of July 6, 2026
item.description:
The week in brief

Foundation models trained on real clinical data posted concrete gains this week. COMPASS, a pan-cancer model built on 10,184 tumors across 33 cancer types, predicted immune checkpoint inhibitor response from bulk tumor transcriptomes, improving average accuracy by 8.5 percent over 22 comparison methods and generalizing to cancer types and treatments not seen during fine-tuning [1]. In neuroimaging, NeuroVFM was trained on 5.24 million clinical magnetic resonance imaging and computed tomography volumes drawn from routine care, an approach its authors call health system learning, and generated radiology reports that outperformed frontier models on accuracy and triage while reducing hallucinated findings [2]. A separate report found that general-purpose large language models can encode electronic health record data into embeddings that match a specialized model across 15 clinical tasks, trading computational efficiency for portability [4].

For educators, these results matter alongside more cautious findings. A retinal fundus model detected dementia with an area under the curve of 0.750 but its authors flagged the need for validation in ethnically diverse populations [3]. Documentation studies examined ambient scribes for intensive care unit rounds [5] and large language model discharge summaries within a secure data environment [6], both framed as feasibility work.

Two items address teaching directly: a student-partnered Delphi study proposing principles for responsible generative AI use [7], and a commentary arguing that model outputs enter clinical settings as information and become evidence only through appraisal and validation [8].

Conference calendar updates

  • ML4H Symposium 2026: start date: TBD is now 2026-12-06; end date: TBD is now 2026-12-07; abstract deadline: TBD is now 2026-09-10; location: TBD is now Sydney, Australia

New opportunities

  • Build with Gemini XPRIZE: newly listed (application deadline 2026-08-17; runs 2026-05-19 to 2026-08-17)
  • Future Caribbean Global Agentic AI Buildathon: newly listed (application deadline Rolling; runs 2026-07-17 to 2026-09-01)

Medical Education

  • Principles for medical students' responsible use of generative AI: a student-partnered Delphi study (PubMed AI in medical education). Delphi study with student partners identifies principles for responsible generative AI use in medical education.

Clinical Practice

  • Generalizable AI predicts immunotherapy outcomes across cancers and treatments (Nature Medicine). COMPASS, a generalizable AI model, predicts immunotherapy outcomes across multiple cancer types and treatments.
  • Health system learning enables generalist neuroimaging models (Nature Medicine). NeuroVFM, trained on routine health system neuroimaging scans, learns general representations that improve diagnosis, report generation, and triage while demonstrating safety advantages of private clinical data.
  • Explainable foundation model for dementia screening and risk stratification using retinal fundus images (npj Digital Medicine). A peer-reviewed study describes an explainable foundation model using retinal fundus images for dementia screening and risk stratification.
  • Large language models are powerful electronic health record encoders (npj Digital Medicine). Peer-reviewed study demonstrates that large language models perform effectively as electronic health record encoders for clinical data extraction and processing.
  • Feasibility of Tailoring Artificial Intelligence, Assisted Ambient Scribes for Intensive Care Unit Rounds: Algorithm Development and Validation (JMIR Medical Informatics). Peer-reviewed study validates feasibility of AI-assisted ambient scribes to reduce documentation burden for intensive care unit physicians.
  • Real-World Implementation of Large Language Models for Writing Clinical Discharge Summaries Within a Secure Data Environment: Development and Expert Evaluation Study (JMIR AI). Large language models generated clinical discharge summaries within a secure data environment, demonstrating real-world implementation for hospital documentation.
  • Fast information and slow evidence in the large language models era (npj Digital Medicine). Article examines how large language models generate fast information but may conflict with slower evidence-based clinical practice.

Videos

  • Sol, Terra, and Luna, our GPT‑5.6 family of models are here. (OpenAI)
  • Listening & Speaking with GPT-Live (OpenAI)
  • Introducing ChatGPT Work, powered by Codex and GPT-5.6 (OpenAI)
  • Tencent HY3 IS REALLY GOOD! Best Open-Weight Model? (FULLY FREE) (WorldofAI)

Podcasts

  • Building Durable AI Agents (Practical AI)

item.title: AUA AI Hub Digest: Week of June 29, 2026
item.description:
The week in brief

A pragmatic, cluster-randomized trial in 16 primary care facilities in Kenya offers some of the first real-world evidence on large language models in low-resource clinical settings. Across 9,691 patients managed by 103 clinical officers, treatment failure within 14 days occurred in 2.2 percent of the group with LLM assistance and 2.0 percent of the control group, a difference that was not statistically significant [2]. The authors report that LLM assistance was safe, with no serious adverse events judged related to the intervention, but that any benefit, if present, is probably modest [2]. This tempers vignette-based comparisons that had suggested LLMs can match or exceed provider performance on some tasks [2].

Several reports this coverage window converge on how models are evaluated. A stress-test study of frontier models such as GPT-5 and Gemini found prevalent brittleness: systems could guess correct answers with key inputs removed yet be confused by minor prompt changes while producing convincing but flawed reasoning traces [3]. An accompanying summary argues that high benchmark scores do not establish readiness for medical decision support or patient-facing use [5]. In authentic oncology notes, GPT-4 produced reasoning errors in 23.1 percent of interpretations, often reflecting cognitive bias patterns; GPT-5.1 reduced error rates but retained the same structured failure patterns [7].

For educators, agent-based systems show measured progress: HemaGuide reported 82.8 percent concordance in a prospective silent trial and hallucinations in 0.3 percent of evaluated cases [4], while agents such as AMIE and MIRA target multi-step clinical problems [1]. A scoping review documents accelerating AI use in undergraduate clinical skills curricula since 2022 [6], and a pilot randomized trial tested prompt-guided virtual microscopy in a histology module [8].

Conference calendar updates

  • ASU+GSV Summit 2027: added to the calendar (2027-04-04 to 2027-04-07, San Diego, California, USA)

Medical Education

  • Artificial intelligence in undergraduate medical education clinical skills curricula: a scoping review of implementations since 2022 (PubMed AI in medical education). Scoping review finds AI implementation in clinical skills education has accelerated substantially since 2022, with emerging applications across simulation and assessment.
  • Turning AI hallucinations into scaffolds for morphologic reasoning: A pilot randomized trial of prompt-guided virtual microscopy learning during a focused histology module (PubMed AI in medical education). Pilot randomized trial tests whether AI-guided prompts in virtual microscopy can scaffold morphologic reasoning skills in medical students.

Clinical Practice

  • Agents AMIE and MIRA advance medical AI capabilities (Nature Medicine). Nature Medicine reports advances in two medical AI agents, AMIE and MIRA, demonstrating improved clinical capabilities.
  • Generative AI-enabled clinical decision support system in primary care: a pragmatic, cluster-randomized trial (Nature Medicine). Pragmatic cluster-randomized trial published in Nature Medicine evaluates a generative AI-enabled clinical decision support system in primary care.
  • Evaluating the robustness and readiness of large frontier models in health AI applications (Nature Medicine). Nature Medicine study evaluates robustness and readiness of large frontier models in health AI applications through adversarial evaluation methods.
  • Clinical decision support in hematological malignancies using a case-grounded AI agent (Nature Medicine). Nature Medicine describes a locally deployable AI agent for clinical decision support in hematological malignancies.
  • Why high scores do not mean application readiness for health AI (Nature Medicine). Nature Medicine article examines why high benchmark scores alone do not predict readiness of large language models for real-world health AI application.
  • Structured reasoning failures compromise LLM interpretation of clinical oncology notes (npj Digital Medicine). Study in npj Digital Medicine shows large language models fail to correctly interpret clinical oncology notes due to reasoning limitations.

Videos

  • MedAI #161: A prospective trial of a conversational AI in a primary care clinic | Jacob Koshy (Stanford MedAI)
  • MedAI #162: Clinical Environment Simulator | Luyang Luo (Stanford MedAI)
  • MedAI #160: AI and LLMs for Advancing Clinical Research and Practice | Rui Zhang (Stanford MedAI)
  • MedAI #159: The Alignment Tax: How the Same Helpful LLMs Training Makes Them Dangerous | Mahmud Omar (Stanford MedAI)

item.title: AUA AI Hub Digest: Week of June 22, 2026
item.description:
Medical Education

  • Clinical Artificial Intelligence Competence in Obstetrics and Gynecology: Patient Safety, Physician Accountability, and Responsible Use (PubMed AI in medical education). Peer-reviewed analysis of clinical AI competence requirements in obstetrics and gynecology, addressing patient safety, physician accountability, and responsible AI use.
  • Construction of a faculty competency model for medical simulation education integrated with GenAI: a multi-method quantitative study based on the perspective of medical students (PubMed AI in medical education). Validated competency model identifies skills and knowledge faculty need to teach medical simulation education integrated with generative artificial intelligence.

Clinical Practice

  • Generative AI-enabled clinical decision support system in primary care: a pragmatic, cluster-randomized trial (Nature Medicine). Pragmatic cluster-randomized trial published in Nature Medicine evaluates a generative AI-enabled clinical decision support system in primary care.
  • Evaluating the robustness and readiness of large frontier models in health AI applications (Nature Medicine). Nature Medicine study evaluates robustness and readiness of large frontier models in health AI applications through adversarial evaluation methods.
  • Automated reanalysis of genomic data for rare disease diagnostics at scale (Nature Medicine). Talos, an automated reanalysis tool, enables genomic data reinterpretation at scale to improve rare disease diagnostic yields over time.
  • Clinical large language model centered on electronic medical records (npj Digital Medicine). Large language model trained on electronic medical records for clinical applications.
  • FetalCLIP: a visual-language foundation model for fetal ultrasound image analysis (npj Digital Medicine). FetalCLIP, a visual-language foundation model for fetal ultrasound image analysis, published in npj Digital Medicine.
  • End to end AI system for surgical gesture sequence recognition and clinical outcome prediction (npj Digital Medicine). Peer-reviewed study presents end-to-end AI system for recognizing surgical gestures in video and predicting clinical outcomes from operative videos.

Videos

  • Improving health intelligence in ChatGPT (OpenAI)

Podcasts

  • More A's, More Fails: What AI Is Really Doing to Student Performance (AI in Education Podcast)
  • AIUC-1: Building trust in AI agents (Practical AI)

item.title: AUA AI Hub Digest: Week of June 15, 2026
item.description:
The week in brief

A head-to-head evaluation in Nature Medicine found that three frontier general-purpose large language models, GPT-5.2, Gemini 3.1 Pro, and Claude Opus 4.6, outperformed two specialized clinical artificial intelligence (AI) tools, OpenEvidence and UpToDate Expert AI, across 500 MedQA questions, 500 HealthBench items, and a 100-query real clinical queries benchmark reviewed by 12 blinded US clinicians [1]. Notably, the clinical tools performed comparably to an auto-enabled Google Search AI Overview on the real-query benchmark, which the authors use to argue for independent, real-world evaluation before tools reach clinical settings [1]. That call for scrutiny connects to a review proposing MedSAFE, a decision-theoretic framework for when a model should abstain rather than answer, since state-of-the-art models still struggle to refuse inappropriate prompts in medical scenarios [6].

For educators, several reports describe AI as a teaching aid rather than a replacement. A meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials found AI-based teaching performed comparably to or better than traditional methods [4], and a quasi-experimental study of 162 students reported a modest gain in self-assessed communication competence after one conversation with a role-prompted GPT-4o chatbot, with a mean difference of 0.94 on a 0-to-10 scale [5]. Spaced repetition work suggests AI can generate flashcards to scale review material in radiology curricula [2].

Workforce preparation drew attention as well, with nursing scholars urging that AI competencies be built into curricula and policy [3]. A podcast episode raises a counterpoint, examining why AI tutoring may correlate with both higher grades and more failures [12].

Medical Education

  • Revolutionizing radiology education: exploring and implementing spaced repetition in radiology teaching and curricula (PubMed AI in medical education). Study evaluates spaced repetition techniques, including Anki platforms, as scalable adjuncts to traditional radiology education and curriculum design.
  • Preparing the nursing workforce for the health AI era: Insights from the Macy Foundation Conference on AI in Medical Education (PubMed AI in medical education). Macy Foundation conference proceedings address curriculum and workforce preparation for integrating artificial intelligence into nursing education and clinical practice.
  • Meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials comparing outcomes of artificial intelligence-based teaching versus traditional-based teaching in medical education (PubMed AI in medical education). Meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials found artificial intelligence-based teaching approaches performed comparably to or better than traditional teaching methods.
  • Training Empathetic Communication Skills in Medical Students With a Role-Prompted GPT-4o Chatbot: Quasi-Experimental Intervention Study (JMIR Medical Education). Quasi-experimental study evaluated a role-prompted GPT-4o chatbot for training empathetic communication skills in medical students with measured learning outcomes.
  • Quantifying the AI readiness gap: An international, multidisciplinary assessment of artificial intelligence literacy in the radiation oncology community (PubMed AI in medical education). International study establishes a global benchmark for artificial intelligence literacy and readiness among radiation oncology clinical staff.
  • Evaluation of Large Language Models in Infectious Disease Decision-Making: From Examination to Clinical Practice (PubMed AI in medical education). Large language models show substantial potential in infectious disease education and decision support, with ability to handle both exam and clinical scenarios.

Clinical Practice

  • General-purpose large language models outperform specialized clinical AI tools on medical benchmarks (Nature Medicine). Nature Medicine reports that general-purpose large language models match or exceed specialized clinical AI tools on medical benchmarks in independent evaluation.
  • When silence is safer: a review and decision-theoretic framework for LLM abstention in healthcare (npj Digital Medicine). Framework proposes decision-theoretic approach for when large language models should abstain from responding in clinical settings.

Videos

  • IBIIS-AIMI Seminar | Susan Shelmerdine, MBBS - Healthcare AI Requires System Changes, Not Just Tasks (Stanford AIMI)
  • Improving health intelligence in ChatGPT (OpenAI)

Podcasts

  • OpenAI’s Karan Singal on HealthBench and the Future of Medical AI (NEJM AI Grand Rounds)
  • More A's, More Fails: What AI Is Really Doing to Student Performance (AI in Education Podcast)

item.title: AUA AI Hub Digest: Week of June 8, 2026
item.description:
The week in brief

A scoping review of 26 studies found that artificial intelligence (AI) tools generally had a positive effect on medical students' clinical education and decision-making during clinical rotations, especially in solving clinical cases and interpreting images, and appeared to help students with less clinical experience reach performance levels closer to those of experienced physicians [1]. The review also reported neutral findings in seven articles, warning that incorrect or unclear AI responses can cause confusion, and it called for structured curricular integration alongside adequate training and regulation [1]. A JMIR Medical Education Viewpoint developed this theme for formative assessment, defining AI-assisted formative assessment as the intentional use of AI tools to support interpretation of performance information for learning rather than grading, while flagging risks including hallucination, automation bias, and epistemic overtrust [4]. The authors note that much of the current evidence remains early, heterogeneous, and concentrated in short-term or single-setting studies [4].

Two empirical reports illustrate both the promise and the limits of large language models (LLMs) in patient-facing roles. A proof-of-concept study found that an LLM constrained to trial-specific documentation produced accurate, readable responses to informed consent questions, but human raters judged some technically accurate answers inadequate because they lacked conversational tone, context awareness, and empathy [2]. Separately, a real-time long short-term memory model predicting inpatient hypoglycemia within 24 hours outperformed baseline models, though its precision was 0.23 at the chosen threshold [3].

For educators preparing students to evaluate such tools critically, benchmarks like MedQA-CS offer an Objective Structured Clinical Examination-style way to assess LLM clinical skills [5].

Conference calendar updates

  • EDUCAUSE Annual Conference 2026: added to the calendar (2026-09-29 to 2026-10-02, Denver, Colorado, USA)

Medical Education

  • EXPRESS: Effect of the Use of Artificial Intelligence Tools on Clinical Education and Clinical Decision-Making of Medical Students During Clinical Rotations: A Scoping Review (PubMed AI in medical education). Medical education is transforming due to new technologies such as artificial intelligence (AI).
  • AI-Assisted Formative Assessment in Clinical Education: From Algorithms to Agency (JMIR Medical Education). Artificial intelligence (AI) is rapidly reshaping clinical education by embedding assessment and feedback into everyday learning activities.
  • MedQA-CS: Objective Structured Clinical Examination (OSCE)-Style Benchmark for Evaluating LLM Clinical Skills (PubMed AI in medical education). MedQA-CS offers an Objective Structured Clinical Examination-style benchmark to evaluate large language model clinical skills accurately.
  • Integrating large language models into prostate cancer training: evidence from comparative benchmarking and a pilot randomized trial (PubMed AI in medical education). A pilot randomized trial shows that teaching with large language models improves prostate cancer training examination performance.
  • Large language models in emergency and critical care medicine: a comprehensive review of applications, challenges, and future directions (PubMed AI in medical education). Emergency and critical care medicine requires the rapid synthesis of heterogeneous clinical data under extreme time constraints.
  • Artificial intelligence in clinical physiology: System-wise applications in diagnostics, monitoring, and medical education (PubMed AI in medical education). CONCLUSION: AI possesses the potential to revolutionize clinical physiology by facilitating predictive, personalized, and participatory healthcare.

Clinical Practice

  • Performance of a large language model in the informed consent process for participation in a clinical trial (npj Digital Medicine). A large language model was evaluated for its performance in guiding patients through the informed consent process for clinical trial participation.
  • Development and prospective evaluation of a real-time deep learning model for inpatient hypoglycemia prediction (npj Digital Medicine). A real-time deep learning model was developed and prospectively evaluated to predict inpatient hypoglycemia events.

Videos

  • Claude Opus 4.8: Lying Machine No More? (Two Minute Papers)
  • Why AI Models Pause to Think: Test Time Compute Explained (IBM Technology)

Podcasts

  • Breaking down the 2026 Stanford AI Index Report (Practical AI)
  • AI for Interoperability in Health Care: Philips’s Carla Goulart Peron (Me, Myself, and AI)
